title: Life Cycle Fundamentals
description: Understand life cycle concepts including phases, gates, states, and transitions. Identify business processes, compare basic and advanced life cycle types, and create life cycle templates.

topics.title: Life Cycle Concepts

content.text: Every product-related object in Windchill must have a life cycle. A life cycle is an automated, graphical model that uses phases and gates to manage objects from initial conceptualization through to obsolescence. It defines the states of maturity for an object, which is distinct from versioning - versioning reflects an object's revision history, while life cycle states describe where the object sits in the business process.
Why Life Cycle States Matter

Life cycle states serve four critical purposes in Windchill. They provide visible status so users can immediately see an object's maturity. They drive business administration by determining which processes and workflows apply. They support data management by controlling how data is organized and progressed. Finally, they enforce access control by governing who can view, modify, or approve the object at each stage.

rows: Creator and team members can modify

content.text: Life cycle maturity and version history address different concerns. An object can be at version B.2 (reflecting two major revisions) while in the In Work state - meaning this particular version is still being developed. Don't confuse a high version number with a released status.
Phases and Gates

Every life cycle state consists of two distinct areas. A phase is a defined period during which specific activities occur - such as design work, review, or testing. A gate is a checkpoint that prevents objects from advancing until certain requirements have been met. While an object is in a gate, it is locked from modification, ensuring data integrity during evaluation.

content.prompt: Match each life cycle concept to its description:

pairs.right: A defined period where specific work activities occur
A checkpoint that locks objects until requirements are met
Represents the maturity level of an object
Reflects the revision history of an object over time

keyTakeaways: Every product-related object in Windchill must have a life cycle that models its progression from conceptualization to obsolescence
Life cycle states drive visible status, business administration, data management, and access control
A phase is a working period for activities, while a gate is a checkpoint that locks objects until criteria are met
Life cycle maturity is distinct from versioning - states reflect business readiness, versions reflect revision history

topics.title: Identifying Business Processes

content.text: Before configuring life cycles in Windchill, you must first assess your current business processes for each data type. This discovery step ensures that the life cycle you build accurately reflects how your organization actually works - rather than imposing a generic template that doesn't fit.
Process Discovery

Effective process discovery involves asking a series of targeted questions for each data type: What events and tasks occur during the object's lifecycle? Who performs each task and what role do they fill? What access permissions are needed at each stage? What criteria must be met before an object can be promoted to the next state? The answers to these questions are best captured in swim lane diagrams or flowcharts that visualize the handoffs between roles.
When identifying major stages of data maturity, focus on the points where access control or responsibilities change. Each of those transition points typically maps to a life cycle state in Windchill.
Four Templates That Define a Business Process

cards.back: Defines the maturity states an object passes through - such as In Work, Under Review, Released, and Obsolete. This is the structural backbone of the business process.
Defines the events, tasks, and routing logic that occur at each stage. Workflows automate the activities that move objects between life cycle states.
Defines the generic roles needed to execute the process - such as Author, Reviewer, and Approver. Team templates are reusable across contexts.
Maps actual participants (real users) to the generic roles defined in the team template for a specific product, library, or project.

content.text: Determine early whether a business process is specific to a single product or library, or whether it applies across the entire organization or site. This decision affects where you create and scope your templates.

content.prompt: Arrange the process discovery steps in the correct order:
items: Identify events and tasks for each data type
Determine who performs each task and their role
Define access permissions needed at each stage

keyTakeaways: Always assess current business processes before configuring life cycles to ensure they reflect real organizational workflows
Process discovery involves identifying events, roles, permissions, and promotion criteria for each data type
Four templates define a complete business process: life cycle, workflow, team, and context team
Scope decisions - whether a process applies to a single product or across the organization - must be made early

topics.title: Life Cycle Types and Properties

content.text: Windchill provides two distinct types of life cycles - Basic and Advanced - each suited to different levels of process complexity. Understanding the differences between them is essential for choosing the right approach when configuring automation for your change processes.
Basic vs. Advanced Life Cycles

rows: Special access control at transition points
Use when standard transitions are insufficient

content.text: Basic life cycles are recommended for most use cases due to their improved performance. Only choose an advanced life cycle when your business process requires workflow-driven movement, company-specific logic, or special access control that cannot be achieved with predefined transitions.
Life Cycle Properties

When creating a life cycle template, you configure several key properties. The Type determines whether it is Basic or Advanced. The Name must be unique because it is used in Object Initialization Rules (OIRs) to assign life cycles to objects. The Object Class should generally remain as wt.fc.WTObject. You can also set whether the life cycle is Enabled or hidden, and whether it is Available for Routing.
Scope and Administration

Life cycle templates are created using the Life Cycle Template Administration utility, which allows you to create, import, export, enable, hide, and delete templates. Templates can be scoped at three levels: Site (system-wide availability), Organization (available within an organizational unit), or a specific application context such as a product, library, project, or program. In Windchill 12.1 and later, you can add new state names through the Customize Modeled Enumerations utility.

content.prompt: Match each life cycle property to its purpose:

pairs.right: Determines whether the life cycle is Basic or Advanced
Must be unique; used in Object Initialization Rules
Controls where the template is available (Site, Org, or context)

keyTakeaways: Basic life cycles use predefined transitions and offer better performance - recommended for most object types
Advanced life cycles use workflows for state movement and are needed only for company-specific processes or special access control
Life cycle names must be unique because Object Initialization Rules reference them to assign life cycles to objects
Templates can be scoped at the Site, Organization, or application context level

topics.title: Life Cycle Transitions

content.text: Transitions are predefined actions that provide the business logic for moving objects between life cycle states. For basic life cycles, transitions are the only mechanism for state movement. Advanced life cycles supplement transitions with workflows, but transitions still form the foundation of state-to-state navigation.
Transition Types

rows: Path used when releasing through a change process
In Work → Released via change notice
Moves to an interim state during promotion
Path for making versions obsolete through change process
Path for releasing to production through change process
Path for promoting or demoting state through promotion request
In Work → Released via promotion request
Under Review → In Work for revision
Path when creating a new version
Released → In Work (new version B.1 created)
Directly sets state using the Set State action

content.text: The Set State transition is unique because it allows multiple destination states from a single source. This gives administrators flexibility to handle exceptional cases without creating a separate transition for each possible path.
Transition Example

Consider an object in the Design state. From Design, a Promote transition can advance it to Released, a Lock transition can move it into Under Review (locking it from modification during evaluation), and a Revise transition creates a new version that starts back in Design. Each transition type encodes specific business logic that controls not just the destination state, but also what happens along the way - such as locking the object, creating a new version, or triggering a change process.

content.prompt: Arrange these transition types from most routine to most exceptional:
items: Revise (create new version for continued work)
Obsolescence (retire a version from active use)

keyTakeaways: Transitions are predefined actions that provide business logic for moving objects between life cycle states
For basic life cycles, transitions are the only means of state movement; advanced life cycles also use workflows
Eight transition types exist - Change, Lock, Obsolescence, Production Released, Promote, Refine, Revise, and Set State - each encoding specific business behavior
Set State transitions uniquely allow multiple destination states from a single source

topics.title: Exercise 1 - Create a Basic Life Cycle Template

content.text: In this exercise, you will import and configure a basic life cycle template for a Development Plan document used by PTC Publications. You will add two new states, reorder the state sequence, and define transition properties to create a complete five-state life cycle.
You must be signed in as bpatterson/ptc and have access to the PTC Publications context to complete this exercise. The Plan Development Life Cycle must be available for import.

content.title: Create a Basic Life Cycle Template
content.objective: Import a life cycle template, add two new phases (Rework and Under Review), reorder states, and define transitions to produce a five-state basic life cycle.

steps.action: Open a browser and click the Windchill bookmark.
steps.detail: This launches the Windchill web client where you will configure life cycle templates.

steps.action: Click Sign In.
steps.detail: You must authenticate before accessing administrative utilities.

steps.action: Sign in to Windchill with username bpatterson and password ptc.
steps.detail: The bpatterson account has the necessary permissions to administer life cycle templates in the PTC Publications context.
steps.hint: Do not save the password when prompted.

steps.action: Browse to Organizations > PTC Publications > Utilities.
steps.detail: The Utilities section contains administrative tools for configuring templates and rules within this organization.
steps.hint: You can also browse to the Templates link and select the Life Cycle Templates view.

steps.action: Select the Life Cycle Template Administration link.
steps.detail: The Life Cycle Template Administration utility is the central tool for creating, importing, exporting, and managing life cycle templates.

steps.action: Import W:\WCCM-AUTO-Lab-Files\Plan Development Life Cycle - INITIAL.zip.
steps.detail: Importing a predefined template gives you a starting point that you will customize with additional states and transitions.
steps.hint: Look for an Import button or action in the administration toolbar.

steps.action: Locate the Plan Development Life Cycle in the Life Cycle Templates table.
steps.detail: Verify the template appears in the table after import, confirming the import was successful.

steps.action: Hover over the Plan Development Life Cycle Type icon and identify the type of life cycle.
steps.detail: The icon tooltip reveals whether the life cycle is Basic or Advanced. This template should be a Basic life cycle.

steps.action: Right-click the Plan Development Life Cycle and select Edit to open the life cycle editor.
steps.detail: Editing a life cycle automatically checks it out. Windchill keeps the history of life cycle editions by creating iterations.

steps.action: In the phase diagram, review the defined states.
steps.detail: Before making changes, understand the baseline configuration — note which states and transitions already exist.

steps.action: Review the life cycle properties: Type (Basic), Description, and Class.
steps.detail: The Object Class enables the association of a life cycle with an object type. The object initialization rules override this setup, so it is recommended to keep the default value wt.fc.WTObject.

steps.action: Click the New Phase icon.
steps.detail: The New Phase icon is in the life cycle editor toolbar and inserts a new phase in the diagram area.

steps.action: From the State menu, select the Rework state from the list of predefined states.
steps.detail: The Rework state will allow objects to cycle back for revision when issues are found during review.
steps.hint: Once the menu is expanded, start typing the state name to avoid scrolling.

steps.action: Add a second new phase and select the Under Review state.
steps.detail: Under Review serves as a gate where objects are locked from modification while stakeholders evaluate them.

tasks.title: Reorder Life Cycle States and Define Transitions for the Creation State

steps.action: Drag the Rework state to follow the Creation state.
steps.detail: State order in the editor reflects the expected flow of the business process. Rework logically follows Creation because objects may need rework before advancing further.
steps.hint: If you encounter an issue, cancel the changes and edit the life cycle again.

steps.action: Drag the Under Review state to follow the Rework state.
steps.detail: Under Review is an interim evaluation state before Release, so it belongs between Rework and Released.

steps.action: In the phase diagram, select Creation.
steps.detail: Selecting a state displays its transition configuration in the Transitions tab below.

steps.action: In the Transitions tab, select the Rework checkbox in the Set State row.
steps.detail: This enables a Set State transition from Creation to Rework, allowing objects to be sent back for rework.

steps.action: Select the Lock checkbox in the Under Review row.
steps.detail: This enables a Lock transition from Creation to Under Review, moving objects into a locked review state.

steps.action: Note the Released checkbox in the Promote row is already selected.
steps.detail: This existing Promote transition allows objects in Creation to be promoted directly to Released.

tasks.title: Define Transitions for the Rework State

steps.action: In the phase diagram, select Rework.
steps.detail: Selecting the Rework state displays its transition configuration options.

steps.action: Select the Creation checkbox in the Revise and Set State rows.
steps.detail: Rework objects can return to Creation for a fresh start via Revise (creating a new version) or Set State (directly changing state).

steps.action: Select the Under Review checkbox in the Lock row.
steps.detail: This allows objects in Rework to be locked and moved into Under Review for evaluation.

steps.action: Select the Released checkbox in the Promote row.
steps.detail: This allows reworked objects to be promoted directly to Released if rework is complete.
steps.hint: Refer to the exercise introduction graphic for additional help.

steps.action: Click Save & Close.
steps.detail: Saving preserves all the state and transition changes you made to the life cycle template.
steps.hint: Review the state diagram one final time before saving to confirm all transitions are correct.

steps.action: Check in the life cycle template to make it available for use.
steps.detail: Checking in makes the template available. The completed life cycle should have five states: Creation, Rework, Under Review, Released, and Obsolete.

keyTakeaways: Life cycle templates can be imported and customized rather than built from scratch
New phases are added using the New Phase icon and selecting from predefined state names
State order in the editor should reflect the expected business process flow
The completed life cycle has five states - Creation, Rework, Under Review, Released, and Obsolete - with transitions defining valid paths between them
